sub_topics.title: Safety in the Immediate Environment
competences: Apply safety in the immediate environment

instructional_blocks.hook: What would you do if you found sharp objects lying on the classroom floor? How can sitting the right way help you feel more comfortable while writing?
teacher_steps: Begin with a story or discussion: Ask learners why it's important to keep the classroom clean and safe. Use simple questions like: 'What happens if we leave pencils and pins on the floor?' 'How can we make sure no one falls or gets hurt in our classroom?'
Show visual aids or a short video (if available) of a clean and organised classroom versus a messy and unsafe one.
Use flashcards or posters to introduce key safety practices, such as keeping materials in the right place and pushing in chairs.
learner_steps: Participate in discussion about classroom safety
Observe visual aids showing safe and unsafe classroom environments
Learn to identify safety practices using flashcards or posters
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Demonstrating Safety Practices in the Classroom. This activity introduces learners to various safety practices within their immediate surroundings (classroom, play grounds) emphasising the importance of a safe and organised environment.

teacher_steps: Divide learners into small groups and assign them simple tasks
One group picks up litter and places it in the bin
Another group arranges books and learning materials
Another group pushes in chairs and organizes tables
Play a fun clean-up song in the background to make it enjoyable
Give stickers or praise to all learners for participating
learner_steps: Work in small groups on assigned cleaning tasks
Pick up litter and place it in bins
Push in chairs and organize tables
Participate in clean-up activities with music
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Hands-on activity where learners practice safety practices in groups. Learners demonstrate how to pick up litter, safely store materials, and arrange furniture neatly. Explanation of why each step is important.

teacher_steps: Show pictures or a short video of a safe and unsafe specialised rooms
Ask learners: 'What is wrong in this picture?' or 'What can happen if we are not careful?'
Explain that safety is about keeping ourselves and others safe and happy in specialised rooms

Introduce safety rules using simple language and actions. For example: 'We walk, not run!' (Demonstrate walking safely), 'We keep our hands away from sharp things.' (Show a blunt object and how to handle it), 'We clean up spills quickly to avoid slipping.' (Pretend to wipe a spill)
Use real objects (toy tools, kitchen utensils, or classroom equipment) to show proper handling
learner_steps: Observe pictures or videos of safe and unsafe specialized rooms
Identify what is wrong in unsafe pictures
Practice safe behaviors like walking (not running), handling objects safely, and cleaning spills
Handle real or toy objects to practice proper usage
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Practising safety rules in specialised rooms. General safety rules include: Clean up spills immediately to avoid slipping. Wash your hands before and after handling food. Turn off all appliances when you are finished using them. Wear protective gear, like gloves, goggles, and closed-toe shoes. Always follow the teacher's instructions. Report any damaged tools, spills, or unsafe situations immediately.

teacher_steps: Divide the class into small groups
Assign each group a rule, such as handling scissors properly or keeping the floor clear
Let them act out the safe way and the unsafe way, then discuss which is better
learner_steps: Act out assigned safety rules showing both safe and unsafe ways
Discuss which approach is better and why
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Role-Play activity for Safe vs. Unsafe Behaviours. Highlight the consequences of not following safety rules.

teacher_steps: Tell a simple story about a character who follows or ignores playground safety rules. Example: 'john saw a broken swing but still played on it. What do you think happened?' Ask: 'What should Tommy have done instead?'
Show images of safe and unsafe playground behaviours (e.g., running near slides, pushing friends, using damaged equipment)
Ask Simple Questions: 'Why should we check for broken swings?' 'What happens if we run near the slide?'

Guide the learners to demonstrate playground safety rules: 'Check for broken equipment before playing' (pretend to inspect swings/slides). 'Wait your turn on the slide' (pretend to line up). 'No pushing or rough play' (demonstrate gentle play). 'Stay away from wet/muddy areas' (pretend to step around a puddle)
learner_steps: Listen to story about playground safety
Observe pictures of safe and unsafe playground behaviors
Demonstrate safety rules through pretend play: checking equipment, waiting turns, gentle play, avoiding hazards
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Practicing safety rules on the playground or play space. Safety rules during play include: Be kind to your friends—no pushing, shoving, or rough games. Take turns when using swings, slides, or other equipment. Hold on tight to the swings and sit properly. Play only in the playground area, not near roads or parking lots. Don't wear anything that might get caught, like scarves or necklaces.

teacher_steps: Take Learners Outside to the Playground for exploration and hazard hunt
Guide them to look for potential hazards (broken swings, wet slides, sharp objects, loose stones)
Ask: 'Is this safe or unsafe?' (Thumbs up for safe, thumbs down for unsafe)
Encourage them to suggest solutions: 'What should we do if we see a broken swing?' (Tell a teacher)
learner_steps: Use thumbs up/down to indicate safe or unsafe items
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Exploration activity where learners hunt for hazards on the playground and discuss the importance of being alert and following rules during play.

teacher_steps: Teach Basic Road Safety Rules for Crossing the Road Safely: STOP: Stand at the edge of the road. LOOK: Look right, look left and right again to check for cars. LISTEN: Listen for any vehicles approaching. THINK: Make sure it's safe before crossing. CROSS: Walk, don't run, cross the road using the pedestrian crossing
Learners to practise crossing the road using a model of a pedestrian crossing or draw a pedestrian crossing on the ground
Discuss the meaning of each traffic light colour: Red Light: Stop! Orange Light (Amber): Get ready to stop! Green Light: Go!
Use flashcards or toy traffic lights to show each colour and let students act out the correct actions
learner_steps: Learn the steps for crossing the road safely: Stop, Look, Listen, Think, Cross
Practice crossing using a model or drawn pedestrian crossing
Learn traffic light colors and their meanings
Act out responses to different traffic light colors
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Demonstrating safety rules on the road. Using Traffic Lights and the 'Stop, Look, and Listen' principle. 1. Stop: Pause before the Curb, Wait for a Safe Gap, Focus on the Task. 2. Look: Check Both Directions (left, right, and left again), Assess All Lanes, Observe Traffic Signals. 3. Listen: Hear Oncoming Traffic, Notice Unusual Noises, Respond to Verbal Warnings.

vocabulary.definition: The state of being out of danger or being safe
The area around us
Rooms like kitchen, art room, music room, computer room, workshop

questions: Why should we keep our classroom clean and tidy?
What should you do if you spill water or food in the classroom?
What should you do before crossing the road?
What should you do if you see someone climbing too high on the playground?

teacher_steps: Display Charts of correct and incorrect posture and videos demonstrating posture techniques in class
Demonstrate sitting correctly: Sit with their back straight and against the chair. Keep both feet flat on the floor. Rest their hands on the desk. Avoid slouching or leaning too far forward
Let learners practice how to sit properly
learner_steps: Observe charts and videos showing correct and incorrect posture
Practice sitting with back straight against chair
Keep both feet flat on the floor
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Performing correct postural habits - Sitting Correctly. Good posture keeps the spine in its natural alignment, reducing strain on muscles and joints. Poor posture, such as slouching, puts pressure on the lower back, leading to pain and discomfort.

teacher_steps: Demonstrate standing correctly: Stand tall with their shoulders back. Keep their chin slightly up and your tummy tucked in. Distribute weight evenly on both feet
Let learners stand like 'soldiers' to practice keeping their bodies straight and tall
learner_steps: Keep chin slightly up and tummy tucked in
Distribute weight evenly on both feet
Practice standing like soldiers - straight and tall
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Standing Correctly. Proper posture opens up the chest and allows the lungs to expand fully. Slouching compresses the lungs, reducing oxygen intake and making breathing less efficient.

teacher_steps: Demonstrate walking properly: Walk with head up and shoulders relaxed. Swing arms naturally by their sides
Have a 'posture parade' where learners practice walking tall like models or superheroes
learner_steps: Walk with head up and shoulders relaxed
Participate in posture parade walking like models or superheroes
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Walking Properly. Good posture prevents backache and improves breathing.

vocabulary.definition: Position and alignment of the body in different activities (standing, sitting, lying down, lifting, kneeling...)

questions: Is the child sitting upright with their back straight and shoulders relaxed?
Are both feet flat on the floor or supported by a footrest?
Is the child's head aligned with their spine and not tilted forward or backwards?
Is the child standing with their weight evenly distributed on both feet?
Are the child's knees slightly bent and not locked?
Are the shoulders level and not slouched or raised?
Is the child walking with a natural gait and not dragging their feet?
Are the arms swinging naturally at the sides?
Is the head held upright, with the chin parallel to the ground?
Does the child bend at the knees and hips rather than the waist?
Is the child using both hands to lift objects, keeping them close to the body?
Does the child avoid twisting their spine while lifting or carrying objects?

instructional_blocks.hook: Have you ever drawn your favorite toy or the house you live in? Imagine being able to create beautiful pictures of all the things you see every day!
teacher_steps: Show learners pictures of the objects (kite, house, car, hat, animal, etc.)
Ask learners to identify and describe the objects
Help develop observation skills and understanding of shapes
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Observing and Identifying objects. This helps develop observation skills and understanding of shapes.

teacher_steps: Provide worksheets with dotted outlines of the objects
Ask learners to trace them
Build confidence and develop hand-eye coordination
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Tracing activity to Build confidence and develop hand-eye coordination.

teacher_steps: Demonstrate step-by-step how to draw the objects on the board
Have learners follow along on paper
Teach the basic shapes and proportions used in sketching
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Guided Drawing to teach the basic shapes and proportions used in sketching.

teacher_steps: Guide learners to draw the objects using basic shapes (e.g., a house with rectangles and triangles, a car with rectangles and circles)
Simplify the drawing process and make it accessible
learner_steps: Use rectangles, triangles, circles to create recognizable objects
Practice simplifying complex objects into basic shapes
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Shape-Based Drawing to simplify the drawing process and make it accessible.

teacher_steps: Tell a simple story that involves the objects (e.g., 'A kite flying in the sky')
Have learners draw scenes from the story
Spark creativity and make the activity engaging
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Draw with Stories to spark creativity and make the activity engaging.

teacher_steps: Ask learners to sketch the objects and colour them using crayons or coloured pencils
Enhance creativity and focus on completing tasks
learner_steps: Color using crayons or colored pencils
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Sketch and Colour to enhance creativity and focus on completing tasks.

teacher_steps: Show an object briefly, then hide it
Ask learners to sketch it from memory
Build memory skills and attention to detail
learner_steps: Draw from memory after object is hidden
Develop memory and attention to detail
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Draw from Memory to build memory skills and attention to detail.

vocabulary.definition: A rough drawing made quickly
The process of making pictures using lines, shapes, and colors
Drawing without the use of tools like rulers or stencils
Things you can see, touch, and use in daily life

teacher_steps: Take learners on a short observation walk around the classroom or school compound
Ask them to look for objects they see daily (e.g., chairs, pots, cups)
Guide learners to name and describe the shape of each object. For example: 'What shape is the cup? Does it have straight or curved edges?'
Display objects like a chair, pot, or cup in the classroom for learners to examine
learner_steps: Name and describe shapes of objects
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Observe and Identify Objects. Drawing various pictures of objects found in the home and school (chairs, pots, cups, money box). Learners confidently identify objects and describe their shapes.

teacher_steps: Demonstrate how to draw an object step by step on the board or a flip chart. For example: Start with the basic outline of a chair (rectangles and straight lines). Add details like the backrest, legs, and seat
Provide each learner with drawing paper and pencils
Let learners attempt to draw a simple object like a cup while the teacher moves around to offer guidance
learner_steps: Draw simple objects with teacher guidance
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Draw (Guided Drawing Activity). Learners practice drawing outlines of objects using simple techniques. Learners create basic outlines of objects with noticeable details.

teacher_steps: Instruct learners to add features to their drawings. For example: Add patterns to a pot (e.g., stripes or flowers). Use crayons to color the chair or money box in creative ways
Encourage learners to think about how the objects look in real life and choose colors accordingly
learner_steps: Add features and patterns to drawings
Think about real-life colors and appearances
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Creative Drawing and Coloring. Learners enhance their drawings by adding details and colors. Learners complete and colorfully decorate their drawings.

teacher_steps: Ask learners to display their completed drawings on the classroom walls or desks
Let each learner explain their drawing, describing what they drew and the details they included
Encourage peers to provide positive feedback, such as: 'I like the color of your chair.' 'Your pot has nice patterns.'
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Peer Sharing and Feedback. Learners share and discuss their drawings with peers. Learners build confidence in explaining their work and develop appreciation for peers' efforts.

teacher_steps: Provide learners with a list of other objects (e.g., pencils, erasers, or bottles)
Ask them to select one and draw it independently
Encourage learners to apply what they've learned (outlines, details, and coloring)
learner_steps: Select an object from provided list
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Practical Application Activity. Learners reinforce their skills by drawing a new object not covered in class.

questions: Can the learner identify the object they have drawn (e.g., chair, pot, cup)?
Does the drawing accurately represent the shape and proportions of the object?
Has the learner included key details that are characteristic of the object?
Does the drawing include all parts of the chair (e.g., seat, backrest, legs)?
Are the legs of the chair evenly spaced and proportional?
Has the learner included any additional features, such as armrests or patterns on the chair?
Does the pot have a clear and well-defined shape (e.g., round, cylindrical)?
Has the learner included the rim and handles (if applicable)?
Are there any decorative elements or textures on the pot?

sub_topics.title: Colouring
competences: Paint pictures based on given themes

teacher_steps: Engage Learners in observing examples or discussing common activities at home (e.g., cooking, eating)
Guide learners to draw pictures depicting these activities
Highlight common features of the activity (e.g., pots and stove for cooking)
learner_steps: Draw pictures showing activities like cooking, eating
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Drawing pictures showing various activities (cooking, eating, people, animals). Highlight common features of the activity (e.g., pots and stove for cooking).

teacher_steps: Guide learners to use green, red, black, and orange paints to recreate the Zambian flag
Explain to the learners the meaning of the colours in the Zambian flag
learner_steps: Use green, red, black, and orange paints
Learn the meaning of flag colors
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Painting a picture of the Zambian flag. Explain to the learners the meaning of the colours in the Zambian flag.

teacher_steps: Guide learners to discuss their favorite animals
Have learners draw them, and paint them using appropriate colors
Focus on familiar animals such as cows, birds, or elephants
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Painting a picture of animals. Focus on familiar animals such as cows, birds, or elephants.

teacher_steps: Guide learners to observe a picture or discuss what happens at a bus station
Have them paint a scene based on this theme
Include elements such as people, buses, and surroundings
learner_steps: Observe picture of bus station or discuss bus station activities
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Painting a bus station scene. Include elements such as people, buses, and surroundings.

vocabulary.definition: Application of colour on a surface

questions: What did you enjoy most about your drawing or painting?
Why did you choose those colours?

instructional_blocks.hook: Have you ever wondered how simple tools can create amazing products like toys or make daily tasks in the kitchen easier? Let's explore the magic behind these tools and how we can use them efficiently!
teacher_steps: Display tools like knives, adzes, and saws
Learners should discuss their names and purposes
Ensure learners understand the unique features of each tool
learner_steps: Discuss the purpose of each tool
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Tool Identification. Learners observe and name various hand tools presented in class. Ensure learners understand the unique features of each tool.

teacher_steps: Provide learners with images or real tools and tasks
Have them match the tool to its correct use
Examples of Matching: Knife → Cutting cardboard. Adze → Carving wood. Saw → Cutting wood
learner_steps: Learn correct applications for each tool
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Matching Tools to Usage. Learners participate in a matching exercise where tools are paired with their specific functions.

teacher_steps: Guide learners in small groups to design and produce a toy using materials like wood or plastic under supervision
Explain the step-by-step process of measuring, cutting, and assembling

Steps to Make a Toy: Choose a Toy Design (e.g., car, boat, or dollhouse), Gather Materials (wood, plastic, or carton boxes), Measure and Cut (use rulers to measure and cut carefully with help), Assemble the Toy (attach parts using glue, nails, or screws), Decorate (paint or color the toy)
learner_steps: Measure and cut with teacher help
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Make Products Using Hand Tools. Learners create simple toys using hand tools.

vocabulary.definition: Tools used manually to perform tasks, such as hammers, screwdrivers, or scissors
machines or devices used to perform a specific task or activity
The act of using something for its intended purpose
The regular care or upkeep of tools, utensils, or equipment to keep them in good working condition
The process of keeping tools, utensils, or other items in a safe and organized place when not in use
The ability to use imagination or original ideas to create or solve problems in new and unique ways

teacher_steps: Show learners common utensils (e.g., spoons, forks, plates, cups, and storage containers)

Let learners briefly say the functions of the common utensils, such as: 'We use a spoon for serving or eating soup.' 'We use a fork to pick up food like rice or vegetables.' 'Plates hold food, and containers help store leftovers.'
Model how to hold and use utensils properly, showing the correct grip and movements
Learners use utensils to simulate serving, eating, and storing food
learner_steps: State the function of each utensil
Practice holding and using utensils correctly
Simulate serving, eating, and storing food
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Use kitchen utensils (e.g. lunchboxes, plates, spoons, cups drinking bottles). Function of each utensil: Spoon for eating or serving soup. Fork for picking up food like rice or vegetables. Plate for holding food. Cup for drinking liquids. Storage container for keeping leftover food fresh. Highlight the ergonomic features of utensils: Comfortable Grip, Adaptable Sizes, User-Friendly Angles.

teacher_steps: Start with a simple discussion: Ask: Why do we need to clean our utensils? Explain in simple terms: Cleaning removes dirt and germs to keep us healthy
Show pictures or real-life examples of clean and dirty utensils
Organize learners into small groups
Give each group utensils to clean
Supervise and guide them as they clean
Encourage teamwork and praise good effort

Show how to clean different utensils: Plastic utensils (e.g., lunchboxes, drinking bottles): Use warm soapy water and a sponge. Metal utensils (e.g., spoons): Use warm water, soap, and a brush if needed. Cups and plates: Show circular motions while scrubbing and rinsing
Explain the steps while demonstrating: Rinse the utensil with water. Apply soap and scrub using a sponge/brush. Rinse thoroughly with clean water. Dry with a clean cloth or place on a drying rack
learner_steps: Follow cleaning steps: rinse, soap, scrub, rinse again, dry
Use proper techniques for different materials
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Clean kitchen Utensils effectively. Demonstrate cleaning techniques for different materials like plastic and metal. Importance of cleanliness for health and safety: Keeps us healthy, Prevents bad smells, Protects our food, Keeps away bugs.

teacher_steps: Ask simple questions: Where do we keep our spoons, plates, and cups at home? Why don't we put them on the floor?
Explain why storage is important: Keeps utensils clean. Prevents breakage or loss. Prevents accidents. Makes it easy to find things when needed
Organize a sorting activity: Provide different utensils. Let learners arrange them in the correct storage places (drawers, cabinets, shelves). Guide and encourage them to store neatly

Show how to store utensils properly: Spoons and forks in a drawer or container. Plates and bowls in a cabinet or rack. Cups and bottles in a safe place, upside down to drain water. Pots and pans on a shelf or hanging hooks
learner_steps: Discuss where utensils are stored at home
Learn why proper storage is important
Arrange utensils in correct storage places
Practice storing items neatly and safely
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Store kitchen utensils in suitable places. Explain why certain storage methods are ideal.

vocabulary.definition: Small tools or instruments used in the kitchen for cooking or food preparation, like spoons, knives, or spatulas

sub_topics.title: Sources of Materials
competences: Identify sources of materials used to make items

instructional_blocks.hook: Do you know where the paper, pots, or chairs in your house come from? Imagine making something special from things around you, like grass or clay!
teacher_steps: Start with a question: 'Have you ever wondered where paper, pots, and leather shoes come from?'
Tell a short story (e.g., about a child who goes to the forest, farm, and pottery workshop and learns how things are made)
learner_steps: Listen to question about material sources
Listen to story about how things are made
Think about where common items come from
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Identifying sources of materials. Start with a question and tell a short story.

teacher_steps: Show learners real objects or picture cards
Ask: 'What is this?' (e.g., hold up paper)
Then ask: 'Where do you think it comes from?' (Guide them to say trees)
Do the same for clay pots (from soil) and leather (from animals)
learner_steps: Guess where each object comes from
Learn that paper comes from trees, pots from soil, leather from animals
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Observing and Identifying. Trees provide wood and paper. Soil is used to make clay products like pots. Animals provide leather and wool.

teacher_steps: Mix up flashcards or real objects
Ask learners to sort them into groups based on their sources (tree, soil, or animal)
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Sorting Game. Mix up flashcards or real objects and ask learners to sort them into groups based on their sources.

teacher_steps: Engage Learners in a matching activity where they pair products with their sources (e.g., matching paper to trees or wool to sheep)
Sing a simple song or chant to reinforce learning (e.g., 'Paper comes from trees, pots from clay, leather from cows, hooray, hooray!')
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Matching products to their sources. Engage Learners in a matching activity.

vocabulary.definition: Things used to make items (e.g., clay, grass, plastic, wood...)
Where materials come from (e.g., trees, soil, animals...)
Items found nearby, like reeds, clay, or grass

questions: Ask learners to name objects and their sources
Let them draw a picture of one object and its source

teacher_steps: Begin with a short story or discussion about why we should take care of materials and the environment. Example: Tell a story about a child who wastes paper, and another who reuses it wisely
Ask simple guiding questions: What happens if we throw everything away? How can we save materials like paper, water, and plastic? Why is it good to plant trees?
Divide learners into small groups and assign each group a simple conservation task
Reusing Paper: Give learners old newspapers or scrap paper. Show them how to use the blank sides for drawing or making crafts
Reducing Waste: learner to use both sides of a paper before throwing it away or how to fold used paper to make bookmarks
Recycling: learners to put used paper or plastic in the recycling bin instead of throwing them away
Planting a Tree or Flower: If possible, let learners plant a small tree or flowers in a designated school area. If planting is not possible, demonstrate using a small potted plant
Encourage learners to talk about what they are doing as they practice conservation methods
learner_steps: Listen to story about material conservation
Answer questions about waste and conservation
Work in small groups on conservation tasks
Practice reusing paper for drawing or crafts
Use both sides of paper before discarding
Plant a tree or flower (if possible)
Talk about conservation methods being practiced
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Demonstrating material conservation methods. Conservation methods include reusing, reducing, recycling, and tree planting.

teacher_steps: Gather learners and ask them to share what they learned: Why is reusing paper good? How does planting trees help the environment? What can they do at home to reduce waste?
Wrap up with a fun song or a simple pledge about taking care of the environment. Example: 'We will reuse, we won't waste, we will plant and keep our place safe!'
learner_steps: Discuss benefits of reusing, planting trees, reducing waste
Participate in conservation song or pledge
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Discussion about conservation learning. Reusing: Turning old materials into new items. Reducing: Using fewer materials to avoid waste. Recycling: Collecting old materials to make new products.

vocabulary.definition: Taking care of materials to avoid waste
Making something new from old materials

questions: Observe if learners participate in reusing, recycling, reducing waste, or planting trees
Ask them to draw a picture of how they saved materials today
Encourage them to tell their parents one way to conserve materials at home

sub_topics.title: Use of Materials
competences: Use locally available materials to make products

teacher_steps: Provide small portions of clay or plasticine for each learner
Show how to knead and soften the clay before shaping
Guide learners to mold simple items such as: Small pots, Animals (elephant, bird, fish...) simple toy car
Encourage creativity while offering support

Highlight the properties of clay: Clay is soft and easy to mold when wet. It hardens when dried or baked. It is used to make pots, bricks, and sculptures. Long ago, people made plates and cups from clay instead of plastic
learner_steps: Mold simple items like pots, animals, or toy cars
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Making products from clay and plasticine. Learners mold simple items like pots or animals using clay or plasticine. Step 1: Preparation - Provide materials and show how to knead. Step 2: Molding - Guide learners and encourage creativity.

teacher_steps: Provide learners with soft grass, reeds, or paper strips (if reeds are unavailable)
Show how to soften and arrange the grass before weaving
Demonstrate a simple weaving pattern (over and under technique)
Start with a small mat or coaster-sized piece
Allow learners to practice, guiding them as needed
Explain the importance of weaving in traditional practices: Making Household Items, Cultural Identity, Clothing and Accessories, Storage and Carrying, Decoration, Teaching Skills
learner_steps: Receive grass, reeds, or paper strips
Learn to soften and arrange materials
Practice over and under weaving technique
Start with small mat or coaster
Create woven item with teacher guidance
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Weaving with grass or reeds. Engage learners in weaving simple mats or baskets. Step 1: Preparation - Provide and prepare materials. Step 2: Weaving Demonstration - Demonstrate simple pattern and allow practice. Weaving is a way of making fabric or other materials by interlacing threads, grass, or reeds. It has been used for many years to create useful household items. Importance includes: Making Household Items, Cultural Identity, Clothing and Accessories, Storage and Carrying, Decoration, Teaching Skills - passed down from elders to young ones, keeping traditions alive.

vocabulary.definition: A soft, colorful, and moldable material used for making shapes and models
A way of making things by crossing materials over and under each other to create mats, baskets, or fabrics

questions: Where does clay come from?
How can we take care of trees and other materials?
What did you make with the clay or grass, and how did you do it?

instructional_blocks.hook: Have you ever noticed how the tiles on the floor, the stripes on your clothes, or the colors in a rainbow repeat in a special way? Today, we will learn how to make our own fun patterns!
teacher_steps: Provide learners with paper and different colors of paint
Guide them to dip their fingers in paint and press them onto the paper in a repeated sequence (e.g., red-blue-red-blue)
Allow them to experiment with different arrangements
Explain: Patterns can be made using repetition of colours or shapes. Fingerprint patterns can be used to create fun images like caterpillars or flowers
learner_steps: Press onto paper in repeated patterns
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Fingerprint Patterns. Patterns can be made using repetition of colours or shapes. Fingerprint patterns can be used to create fun images like caterpillars or flowers.

teacher_steps: Give learners crayons or pencils and paper
Demonstrate how to create patterns by making repeated scribbles or lines (zigzag, wavy, straight, etc.)
Let them practice creating their own unique line-based patterns
Explain: Lines and scribbles can be arranged in sequences to create patterns. Patterns help in early writing and drawing skills
learner_steps: Receive crayons or pencils and paper
Create repeated scribbles or lines (zigzag, wavy, straight)
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Scribble and Line Patterns. Lines and scribbles can be arranged in sequences to create patterns. Patterns help in early writing and drawing skills.

teacher_steps: Provide learners with brushes and paint
Show them how to splash paint onto paper to create random yet repeating designs
Encourage them to experiment with different colors
Explain: Splashed paint forms abstract patterns. This method encourages free artistic expression
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Splash Patterns. Splashed paint forms abstract patterns. This method encourages free artistic expression.

teacher_steps: Provide learners with colored paper and glue
Guide them to tear paper into small pieces and arrange them in repeating sequences (e.g., red-yellow-red-yellow)
Help them glue their torn pieces onto another paper to form a pattern
Explain: Tearing paper helps develop fine motor skills. Different colors and shapes can be used to make attractive designs
instructional_blocks.short_notes: Tear Patterns. Tearing paper helps develop fine motor skills. Different colors and shapes can be used to make attractive designs.

vocabulary.definition: A repeated design, shape, or sequence
A mark left by the tip of a finger
A freehand drawing made with random lines
A repeated design made using straight or curved lines
A design created by splattering paint or ink
A design made by tearing pieces of paper and arranging them in a repeated sequence

questions: What happens if we change one color in our pattern?
Can you show me a pattern using your hands?
